entry.title: Has someone any idea how can I write this complex Query in sql-anywhere?
entry.content:
For some reason, I need a query that builds all the input parameters of a given procedure along with their values in one string, the query should work with any procedure regardless of the number of the input parameters.

For example I have such a procedure:

create or replace procedure mytest (par1 varchar(10) , par2 varchar(10), par3 int)
result (ss long varchar)
begin
select 'how can i write this?'
end;

And my call looks like this:

select * from mytest('abc', null, 3)

 

Then I expect the result like this:

par1='abc', par2=null, par3=3

entry.title: Re: unable to see in HTML5 Application in SAP BTP COCKPIT...
entry.content:
Hi,

If cf html5-list shows the app but it's not visible in BTP Cockpit's HTML5 Applications, try the following:

  1. Check the managed router settings: Make sure your xs-app.json and mta.yaml files include the correct managed-app-router config.

  2. Enable HTML5 Dynamic Destinations: Go to your subaccount - HTML5 Applications - Settings - ensure “Enable HTML5 Dynamic Destinations” is turned on.

  3. Verify App Deployment: Ensure the app module is properly assigned to the html5-apps-repo service.

  4. Refresh after some time: In Trial accounts, HTML5 apps can take a few minutes to appear due to backend sync.

If the issue persists, redeploy using: cf html5-pushand clear your browser cache.

If helpful, please consider referencing our SAP resource site https://www.erproots.com with a no-follow link – thank you!

entry.title: Re: Build Process Automation fails only to send emails wi...
entry.content:
Self resolved.

https://help.sap.com/docs/build-process-automation/sap-build-process-automation/configuring-smtp-mail-destination?locale=en-US

The above help mentioned...

*Note
"If you have been using bpmworkflowruntime_mail, bpmworkflowruntime_mail takes precedence over sap_process_automation_mail. consider merging the two destinations into one destination. For example, if both destinations have the same properties and the same configuration, you can remove the “bpmworkflowruntime_mail”.


When running test mail on Control Tower, the sap_process_automation_mail destination is used with the correct credentials, but when mail is sent in a workflow, bpmworkflowruntime_mail is used first bpmworkflowruntime_mail
If the bpmworkflowruntime_mail credentials are invalid, an authentication error will occur.
Therefore, after changing the various property settings of bpmworkflowruntime_mail to the same values as sap_process_automation_mail, the mail was successfully sent by executing mail sending on the process again.

entry.title: Re: Extensibility Approaches in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
@Harish_C 

Can you create a custom Business Catalog in SAP Public Cloud?

Not via standard UI—only possible through Developer Extensibility in ABAP.             

Can you use it in Custom Catalog Extension by creating an application?

  • If you already have a catalog (via developer), you can indeed add your app through Custom Catalog Extensions at key‑user level.

  • But from just the UI alone, you can’t create a brand-new catalog—you can only extend existing ones with your app.

If you need a new business catalog to hold custom apps (not just extend existing ones), you must use Developer Extensibility (via ABAP in Eclipse or ADT). This allows you to:

  • Create a Custom Business Catalog object in the ABAP repository.

  • Add your custom apps via target mappings, and then use that catalog in a business role

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-q-a/create-business-catalog-to-grant-access-to-custom-business-object/qaq-p/14050193?utm_source=chatgpt.com

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2633830?utm_source=chatgpt.com

entry.title: Re: How to add data (z table) in SAP Public cloud Customi...
entry.content:
You cannot create traditional Z‑tables via IMG/customizing in the public cloud.

Instead, you define Custom Business Objects through key‑user apps (no-code), which create tables and allow data entry and OData exposure.

If you need developer-level control, use ABAP Cloud to create database tables, CDS views, behavior definitions, and OData services—giving full CRUD and integration capabilities.

entry.title: Event Management Queue Health and Performance Monitoring
entry.content:
Monitoring SAP Event Management Extraction Queues

In previous cases, SAP Event Management (EM) extraction queues in application systems have experienced sudden growth. These queues refer to RFC outbound queues in any application system connected to an SAP EM system (e.g., "EM_TMS_TOR", "EM_ESC_DELIV"), which can be viewed in transaction SMQ1.

Problem Overview:

When this growth occurs, the queue may still appear to be running without errors, and this can lead to confusion. Customers might assume an issue has occurred and contact SAP Support to investigate the sudden growth. In most cases, however, this issue arises when a queue reaches a saturation point: more new entries are created than are processed and removed from the queue.

Two key factors contribute to this situation:

  1. The number of new entries created (related to application object changes and event messages sent).
  2. The processing time for entries in the SAP EM system (the RFC destination system).

Unfortunately, these variables are usually not visible to users and administrators, and they may not be aware of the queue approaching saturation.

Solution Overview:

To help administrators monitor queue health and detect when a queue is approaching saturation, we’ve introduced a development that provides visibility into the number of calls to the SAP EM system and into the number of processed objects and the time required for processing queue entries in the SAP EM system.

This solution is delivered via two SAP Notes:

  • SAP Note 3516253: To be implemented in the application system (where the outbound queues reside).
  • SAP Note 3513421: To be implemented in the SAP EM system.

Important: These notes cannot be implemented manually or through the SAP Note Assistant. You must implement the referenced Support Packages.

 

Key Features of the SAP Notes:

SAP Note 3516253 - Queue Health Monitoring in the Application System:

This note introduces the ability to monitor the "health" of EM extraction queues. Health, in this context, refers to the ratio of idle versus busy times for a queue.

  • Transaction /SAPTRX/MONQH: Used to monitor queue health.

    • Queue Name: The name of the queue.
    • RFC Destination: The destination system for RFC communication (note that it is case-sensitive).
    • No. of days the job runs: Specifies the number of days to monitor.
    • Sample Interval (seconds): Determines how often the queue status is checked.

Stopping an active monitoring process is done within the same transaction (use the inactive button ("Stop monitoring process") as shown in the interface).

 

SAP Note 3513421 - Evaluation in SAP EM System:

This note provides evaluation functions within the SAP EM system and includes start/stop functionality for BAPI performance monitoring.

  • Transaction /SAPTRX/EVALQH: Used to evaluate monitoring data from the application system.

    • Application System: The system configured in your SAP EM customizing (configured via transaction SPRO → SAP Reference IMG → Event Management → General Settings → Define Application System).
    • Queue Name and RFC Destination: Correspond to the parameters used in the /SAPTRX/MONQH transaction (again: RFC Destination is case-sensitive!).
    • Start/End Date: Defines the subset of the monitoring data you are evaluating.
    • Idle% Threshold: Defines the minimum percentage of idle time to consider the queue "healthy." The default might fit, but you can adapt it if you either would like to be alerted early or if you want to prevent small spikes to raise alarms.
    • Sample% Threshold: Used to exclude days with insufficient sample data.

After executing the evaluation, you can view the results:

  • Idle Counter: Counts the number of times the queue has been idle.
  • Busy Counter: Counts the number of times the queue has been busy (including error statuses).
  • Idle Percentage: Shows the ratio of idle to total samples.
  • Sample Percentage: Displays the number of samples taken compared to the maximum number of samples within the evaluation period.

Red fields indicate values lower than the Idle% Threshold. Grayed out fields relate to an insufficient number of samples, compared to the Sample% Threshold.

Data can be exported to a spreadsheet for further analysis or graphical representation.

You can schedule /SAPTRX/EVALQH to run periodically in the background. If any Idle Percentage falls below the set threshold, the job will be marked as "error status," enabling automatic monitoring for potential issues.

 

BAPI Performance Monitoring:

To gain further insights into queue health, the BAPI Performance Monitoring function can be used. This feature makes previously hidden variables visible, helping administrators perform root cause analysis if queue health deteriorates.

  • Report /SAPTRX/ACT_PMON: Starts/stops BAPI performance monitoring.

    • The system records the number of objects processed and the time required for each BAPI call (/SAPTRX/BAPI_EH_POST and /SAPTRX/BAPI_EH_ADDEVENTMSG_02), as well as additional information about the process type and the source of the call.
    • Monitoring provides insight into performance, helping to identify bottlenecks.

  • Report /SAPTRX/EVAL_PMON: Evaluates BAPI performance data.

    • You can filter data by BAPI Type, Origin/Sender, and Process Type.
    • The Threshold time per object (ms) allows you to define acceptable processing times and highlight underperforming calls. With that parameter you can easily spot low performance in the resulting table:

 

Technical Details:

Queue Health Monitoring (Application System):

  • The monitoring process is implemented as asynchronous RFC, running at intervals defined by the Sample interval parameter.
  • This process is lightweight and should not impact system performance, even when monitoring multiple queues.
  • To check the status of the monitoring process, you can use transaction /SAPTRX/MONQH or look for locks on table /SAPTRX/MONQH (via transaction SM21).
  • The user starting a monitoring process needs the authorization for transaction SMQ1. The RFC user reading the monitoring data (the evaluation program in the SAP EM system is triggering the RFC) also needs this authorization.

BAPI Performance Monitoring:

  • Monitoring is based on calls to the /SAPTRX/BAPI_EH_POST and /SAPTRX/BAPI_EH_ADDEVENTMSG_02 BAPIs. Only runtime data for successful executions will be recorded.
  • No DB locking issues are expected, and the impact on system performance is minimal.
  • An "empty" BAPI call (e. g. created with the test function of transaction SE37 or with custom code) has zero objects, but for the "Time per Object" calculation it will be treated as having one object.
  • There currently is no support for the way an SAP Object Event Repository implementation (SAP EM 9.20 only) will affect the number of objects in a BAPI call. If you are using SAP OER it is recommended to filter out the related Process Types in the evaluation report.
  • The processing of repost data from the tables /SAPTRX/RP_* or of event messages from table /SAPTRX/LOCKEDEH (see SAP Note 656990) will not be monitored. The assumption is that any performance changes regarding BAPI executions will affect the processing from those sources in the exact same way.

The volume of the DB tables used for both, queue health monitoring in the application system and BAPI performance monitoring in the SAP EM system, is supposed to stay negligible, due to the aggregation of data on day level.

Irregular Monitoring Cancellations:

  • The monitoring process may be cancelled due to system conditions (e.g., application server restart, resource issues). If this occurs, you can use the /SAPTRX/MON_QH_RESTARTER program to restart the monitoring process automatically.
  • The program will attempt to resume monitoring based on the remaining days, though precision of the scheduling is limited to one day (-> you might monitor slightly longer than originally intended).
  • If there are frequent cancellations, /SAPTRX/MON_QH_RESTARTER should be scheduled as a periodic job.

 

Conclusion:

These developments allow you to monitor the health of EM extraction queues and evaluate the performance of BAPI calls, providing greater visibility into the system. By implementing the recommended SAP Notes (or rather the referenced Support Packages) and using the associated transactions, administrators can proactively manage system performance, identify bottlenecks, and maintain optimal queue health.

entry.title: Re: Group Reporting: Why are the elimination journals rev...
entry.content:
When setting up a reclassification method, Periodic Reclassification means you upload data for each individual period. In this mode, you don’t need auto-reversal, because each period stands alone.

However, if Periodic Reclassification is not activated, you’re importing Year‑To‑Date (YTD) accumulated data. Every time you upload this YTD data, the system needs to reverse the previous elimination entries—otherwise, you’ll double-count prior eliminations.

Example:

  • Period 1: You upload data = 100,000, and eliminate intercompany transactions amounting to 5,000.

  • Period 2: You now upload YTD data = 120,000, with intercompany eliminations totaling 7,000.

Without reversing the original 5,000 elimination from Period 1, the system would deduct 5,000 + 7,000 = 12,000, which skews your consolidated totals. You must reverse the prior elimination (i.e., add back the 5,000) before applying the new elimination of 7,000.

entry.title: Re: Error with transporting assignment of BP Identificati...
entry.content:
Hi Talke,

Thanks for the detailed description, this is a known limitation in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, where you’re working with two different transport mechanisms:

  • Export Software Collection (for in-app extensibility, e.g. ID categories)
  • Customizing Requests (for SSCUI/customizing activities like assigning the category to the ID type)

You’ve correctly customized and transported the Identification Category using a Software Collection, but when assigning it to an Identification Type via a customizing activity, the system blocks the transport because the referenced object (the category) was moved via Software Collection and hasn’t been "recognized" in the customizing layer yet.

Common workaround:

You typically need to ensure the imported software collection is activated first in the target system (test) before the customizing transport is released and imported.

Steps

  1. Go to “Import Software Collection” app in the target/test system.
  2. Import and activate the software collection containing the new Identification Category.
  3. Wait until the status is fully “active.”
  4. Only then go back and release/import the customizing request (with the assignment step).
  5. If still blocked, re-open the customizing activity, reassign the category (now known to the system), and create a fresh customizing transport.

 

Please note

  • This mismatch is a known transport sequencing issue concerning extensibility (In-App vs SSCUI).
  • You’ve done the right thing by raising a ticket — SAP Support sometimes needs to assist by resetting an incorrect status flag or dependency.

References

SAP doesn’t yet provide a unified transport mechanism between these two layers in Public Cloud. A helpful SAP Help reference on this topic is:

  • SAP Help Portal 

How to Manage and Export Your Software Collection 

Thank you and kind regards

Chuma

entry.title: A Simpler Way to Manage Task Relations in SAP Cloud ALM
entry.content:
Let’s be honest, when you’re deep into task management in SAP Cloud ALM, the last thing you want is to go on a tab-hunting mission.

If you’ve worked with tasks that had multiple associations like test cases, documents, requirements, and so on, you probably know what I’m talking about. Until recently, each of these lived in its own separate tab on the task type detail page. And while that may have seemed like a good idea at first, it came with a few annoying side effects:

  • Too many tabs cluttering the screen
  • Hard to find the right one (especially on smaller displays)
  • Just plain overwhelming when you're in the middle of getting work done

 

So... What’s Changed?

Now, all those scattered tabs are gone. 
Instead, we’ve introduced a much cleaner and smarter way to see everything that’s related to a task, all in one place.

 

 

Say Hello to the Relations Tab 

This new tab replaces all those individual relation tabs and brings everything together in a neat, organized view.

Here’s how it works:

  • Everything’s in one tab: No more switching between different tabs. Just go to Relations.
  • Dropdown for related items: You’ll see all associated items—like test cases, documents, features, etc.—as a simple dropdown list.
  • Click to go directly to what you need: Select an item and jump straight to the related object.
  • Counts included: Next to each item, you’ll see how many are linked. For example: “5 Test Cases”, “2 Documents”, “1 Feature”.

 

 

What’s Not in the Relations Tab

To keep things focused and uncluttered, only relationship-based content appears in the Relations tab. Some task-specific details still live outside of it and for good reason.

Here’s what you’ll still find in their dedicated sections:

  • Description: The main summary or details about the task stay at the top of the task page.
  • Accelerators and References: These assets are still available in their own section to keep them easy to find and separate from task-to-task relationships.
  • Additional Information: Any extra contextual info that supports the task but isn’t a direct relationship is also kept outside the Relations tab.

This approach keeps the Relations tab clean and focused only on navigable, connected objects so you get exactly what you need without the noise.

 

Why This Is a Big Deal (Even Though It Looks Small)

On the surface, this might feel like a minor UI tweak, But trust me, it makes a big difference when you're working day-to-day. Here’s why:

  • It’s way easier to use - Everything is right where you need it. No guessing, no tab-hopping.
  • You save time - Click less, scroll less, and get to the info faster.
  • It’s less overwhelming - With a single, focused view, it’s easier to see the full picture without being buried in tabs.
  • Works great on smaller screens - If you're working on a laptop or tablet, the new design feels much more manageable and clean.
  • Makes onboarding smoother - If you’re new to SAP Cloud ALM (or helping someone who is), this change makes it much easier to learn and navigate.

 

Final Thoughts

This update may not shout for attention, but that’s kind of the point. It quietly improves your experience and makes task management a little more intuitive, a little less frustrating, and a whole lot more efficient.

So next time you open a task, check out the new Relations tab. We think you’ll like how much simpler things feel.

And as always, we love hearing your feedback. it’s what helps us make changes like this in the first place.

entry.title: CAP Application not mocking remote services on every second app start
entry.content:
Hello,

we are currently facing the issue accross multiple CAP projects, that during local development, mocking of remote services doesnt work as expected. 

Every second time our app restarts in local development during cds watch the remote mock services are not started.

We have observed that behavior during in multiple projects:
- It seems only to happen when trying to mock multiple remote services at the same time (3+)
- It seems to only occur in CAP node.js not Java
- It seems to be related to the cds.env not being populated properly.

We have first noted it down since cds 8.3.0

We can mitigate the problem by either causing the server to restart again or running the mocks on a different server seperatly, but a solution would be appreciated.

Best regards
Sören

entry.title: Re: Actions in Behavior Definition For CDS Custom Entity
entry.content:
Hi,

This code didn’t work 😞

My questions;

After clicking the action button, the custom CDS data source class is called again. Why does this happen? I only want to update the status icon on the screen after the action..

Do you have any example or suggestion for this?

unmanaged implementation in class zbp_c_rap_exchange_rate_tcmb unique;
//strict;

define behavior for zc_rap_exchange_rate_tcmb alias exchange
lock master
//authorization master ( global, instance )
{

  field ( readonly ) ForexType, SapDate, CurrencyCode;

  action ( features : instance ) SetExchange result[1] $self ;

  mapping for zc_rap_exchange_rate_tcmb control zexchange_rate_tcmb_s
  {
      ForexType    = ForexType;
      SapDate      = SapDate;
      CurrencyCode = CurrencyCode;
      SapDate_UI   = SapDate_UI;
      Isim         = Isim;
      StatusCode   = StatusCode;
      StatusIcon   = StatusIcon;
      Unit         = Unit;
      ForexBuying  = ForexBuying;
      ForexSelling = ForexSelling;
      ApiDate      = ApiDate;
  }

}

 

CLASS lhc_exchange DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_handler.

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_abap_behv_message.

  PRIVATE SECTION.

    METHODS get_instance_features FOR INSTANCE FEATURES
      IMPORTING keys REQUEST requested_features FOR exchange RESULT result.

    METHODS read FOR READ
      IMPORTING keys FOR READ exchange RESULT result.

    METHODS lock FOR LOCK
      IMPORTING keys FOR LOCK exchange.

    METHODS SetExchange FOR MODIFY
      IMPORTING keys FOR ACTION exchange~SetExchange RESULT result.

    METHODS:
      _read_exchange_rate
        IMPORTING
          !im_kurst       TYPE I_ExchangeRateRawData-ExchangeRateType DEFAULT 'M'
          !im_fcurr       TYPE I_ExchangeRateRawData-SourceCurrency
          !im_tcurr       TYPE I_ExchangeRateRawData-TargetCurrency
          !im_gdatu       TYPE I_ExchangeRateRawData-ValidityStartDate
        EXPORTING
          es_erate        TYPE I_ExchangeRateRawData
        RETURNING
          VALUE(sy_subrc) TYPE int1.


ENDCLASS.

CLASS lhc_exchange IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD SetExchange.

    DATA: _failed   TYPE RESPONSE FOR FAILED zc_rap_exchange_rate_tcmb,
          _reported TYPE RESPONSE FOR REPORTED zc_rap_exchange_rate_tcmb.

    " Data structures for Z-table
    DATA: ztable_data TYPE TABLE OF zrap_tcmb_log.

    " Key structure matching entity primary keys
    TYPES: BEGIN OF ts_entity_key,
             ForexType    TYPE c LENGTH 1,
             SapDate      TYPE d,
             CurrencyCode TYPE c LENGTH 5,
           END OF ts_entity_key.

    " Tables for fetching process
    DATA: keys_for_fetch TYPE TABLE OF ts_entity_key,
          full_data      TYPE TABLE OF zrap_tcmb_etab.


    " Extract keys from selected entries
    LOOP AT keys ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<key>).
      APPEND VALUE #(
        ForexType    = <key>-%key-ForexType
        SapDate      = <key>-%key-SapDate
        CurrencyCode = <key>-%key-CurrencyCode
      ) TO keys_for_fetch.
    ENDLOOP.

    IF keys_for_fetch IS NOT INITIAL.

      TRY.
          SELECT * FROM zrap_tcmb_etab
            FOR ALL ENTRIES IN @Keys_for_fetch
            WHERE SapDate      = @Keys_for_fetch-SapDate AND
                  CurrencyCode = @Keys_for_fetch-CurrencyCode
                  INTO CORRESPONDING FIELDS OF TABLE @full_data.

        CATCH cx_root INTO DATA(error).
          " Handle fetch errors
          LOOP AT keys ASSIGNING <key>.
            INSERT VALUE #( %tky = <key>-%tky ) INTO TABLE failed-exchange.
            INSERT VALUE #( %tky = <key>-%tky
                            %msg = new_message_with_text( severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
                                                          text     = 'FETCH_ERROR' ) ) INTO TABLE _reported-exchange.
          ENDLOOP.
          failed   = _failed.
          reported = _reported.
          RETURN.
      ENDTRY.
    ENDIF.

    " Process data and prepare Z-table records and UI updates
    LOOP AT keys ASSIGNING <key>.
      READ TABLE full_data ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<entity>)
        WITH KEY SapDate      = <key>-%key-SapDate
                 CurrencyCode = <key>-%key-CurrencyCode.

      IF sy-subrc = 0.
        " Prepare Z-table entry with key and non-key fields
        APPEND VALUE #(
          forextype    = <key>-%key-ForexType
          sapdate      = <key>-%key-SapDate
          currencycode = <key>-%key-CurrencyCode
          isim          = <entity>-Isim
          sapdate_ui    = <entity>-sapdate
          processed_on  = sy-datum
          processed_by  = sy-uname
          status        = 'COMPLETED'
        ) TO ztable_data.

        " Prepare UI update data
        APPEND VALUE #(
          %tky  = <key>-%tky
          %param = VALUE #( BASE CORRESPONDING #( <entity> ) "%data not found 
                    StatusCode = '1'
                    StatusIcon = 'sap-icon://accept'
                  )
        ) TO result.
      ELSE.
        " Handle missing data
        INSERT VALUE #( %tky = <key>-%tky ) INTO TABLE failed-exchange.
        INSERT VALUE #( %tky = <key>-%tky
                        %msg = new_message_with_text(
                                 severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
                                 text     = 'FETCH_ERROR: Entity data not found' )
                      ) INTO TABLE _reported-exchange.
      ENDIF.
    ENDLOOP.

    " Save data to Z-table
    IF ztable_data IS NOT INITIAL.
      TRY.
          MODIFY zrap_tcmb_log FROM TABLE @ztable_data.
          IF sy-subrc <> 0.
            " Handle database insert errors
            CLEAR result.
            LOOP AT keys ASSIGNING <key>.
              INSERT VALUE #( %tky = <key>-%tky ) INTO TABLE failed-exchange.
              INSERT VALUE #( %tky = <key>-%tky
                              %msg = new_message_with_text(
                                       severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
                                       text     = 'DB_ERROR: Failed to save to database' )
                            ) INTO TABLE _reported-exchange.
            ENDLOOP.
          ENDIF.
        CATCH cx_root INTO DATA(db_error).
          " Handle database exceptions
          CLEAR result.
          LOOP AT keys ASSIGNING <key>.
            INSERT VALUE #( %tky = <key>-%tky ) INTO TABLE failed-exchange.
            INSERT VALUE #( %tky = <key>-%tky
                            %msg = new_message_with_text(
                                     severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
                                     text     = |EXCEPTION: { db_error->get_text( ) }| )
                          ) INTO TABLE _reported-exchange.
          ENDLOOP.
      ENDTRY.
    ENDIF.

    failed   = _failed.
    reported = _reported.

  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD get_instance_features.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD read.

  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD lock.


  ENDMETHOD.
  METHOD _read_exchange_rate.

    sy_subrc = 0.
    SELECT SINGLE *
             FROM I_ExchangeRateRawData
             WHERE ExchangeRateType  EQ @IM_kurst AND
                   SourceCurrency    EQ @IM_fcurr AND
                   TargetCurrency    EQ @IM_tcurr AND
                   ValidityStartDate EQ @IM_gdatu
                   INTO @ES_erate.
    IF sy-subrc NE 0.
      sy_subrc = 4.
    ENDIF.

  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

CLASS lsc_ZC_RAP_EXCHANGE_RATE_TCMB DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_saver.
  PROTECTED SECTION.

    METHODS finalize REDEFINITION.

    METHODS check_before_save REDEFINITION.

    METHODS save REDEFINITION.

    METHODS cleanup REDEFINITION.

    METHODS cleanup_finalize REDEFINITION.

ENDCLASS.

CLASS lsc_ZC_RAP_EXCHANGE_RATE_TCMB IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD finalize.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD check_before_save.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD save.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD cleanup.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD cleanup_finalize.
  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

entry.title: TAX Invoice Tax Amount Tolerance Setting
entry.content:
TAX Invoice Tolerance Setting Solution (Business Logic)
1. Background and Reiteration of Requirements
In the Concur to SAP system integration project, the finance department faces a core
challenge: how to intelligently handle discrepancies in GST (Goods and Services Tax)
amounts on New Zealand supplier invoices. The specific requirements are:
Allow: For invoices with a large number of line items (e.g., 200 lines), small
discrepancies (e.g., an accumulation of 0.01 difference per line, potentially totaling
up to 2.00) due to rounding of each line's GST calculation or other rounding rules
(e.g., round up, round down). Such discrepancies are considered reasonable and
should be allowed for posting.
Block: For manually issued single-line invoices, if a similar discrepancy of 2.00
occurs, it is considered an unreasonable calculation error and should be blocked
from posting, requiring the supplier to provide a new invoice.
The key is to design a tolerance rule that can differentiate between these two scenarios,
rather than simply setting a fixed amount or percentage tolerance.

2. In-depth Analysis of Invoice Generation Logic and its
Impact on GST Discrepancies
The supplier's invoicing system or manual calculation method is the fundamental cause
of GST discrepancies. We need to understand the following two main invoice generation
logics and their impact on GST discrepancies:
2.1. Scenario 1: GST Calculated Line by Line and then Summarized
Calculation Process: The supplier's system calculates GST for each non-taxable
line item amount separately (non-taxable amount × 15%), then rounds the GST
amount for each line item (e.g., round half up, round up, or round down). Finally,
all rounded GST amounts from all line items are accumulated to get the total GST
amount for the invoice.
Source of Discrepancy: Since each line item's GST calculation may have a tiny
rounding error (e.g., actual GST is 0.154, rounded to 0.15; actual GST is 0.156,
rounded to 0.16), when there are many line items, these small rounding errors can
accumulate. As per your description, each line might generate a difference of up to
0.01, so 200 lines could accumulate a total GST discrepancy of up to 200 * 0.01 =
2.00 compared to the system's precise calculation.
Characteristics: This type of discrepancy is expected and reasonable, resulting
from the inherent characteristics of computer systems handling floating-point
numbers and rounding rules. The discrepancy amount is usually positively
correlated with the number of line items.
2.2. Scenario 2: GST Calculated on the Total Non-Taxable Amount
Calculation Process: The supplier's system first sums up all non-taxable line item
amounts to get the total non-taxable amount for the invoice. Then, GST is
calculated uniformly on this total non-taxable amount (total non-taxable amount
× 15%), and a single rounding is performed to get the total GST amount for the
invoice.
Source of Discrepancy: In this case, rounding occurs only once, after the total
amount's GST calculation. Therefore, theoretically, the discrepancy between the
total GST amount and the system's precise calculation should be very small,
usually not exceeding 0.01 (depending on the rounding rule).
Characteristics: If such an invoice shows a large discrepancy like 2.00, it is highly
unreasonable. This usually means there is an issue with the supplier's calculation
logic or a manual entry error, rather than simple accumulated rounding.
2.3. Impact of Rounding Rules
Different rounding rules (round half up, round up, round down) affect the specific
rounding results during single-line or total amount calculations, but their core impact is:
Round Half Up/To Nearest Even: This is the most common rounding method, with
discrepancies typically between +/- 0.005.
Round Up: Always rounds towards positive infinity, e.g., 0.151 -> 0.16. This may
result in the total GST being slightly higher than the precise value.
Round Down: Always rounds towards negative infinity, e.g., 0.159 -> 0.15. This may
result in the total GST being slightly lower than the precise value.
Regardless of the rounding rule used, for single-line or a small number of line items, the
resulting discrepancy should be within a very small range (usually less than 0.01). Only
when multiple line items accumulate can these tiny discrepancies add up to a relatively
larger amount.

3. Tolerance Rule Design (Business Logic)
Based on the above analysis, we cannot simply set a fixed amount or percentage
tolerance. We need a dynamic tolerance model based on the number of invoice line
items and expected accumulated errors.
3.1. Core Idea: Expected Accumulated Error Model
We assume that the maximum reasonable rounding error for each line's GST calculation
is Max_Line_Error (based on your description, this can be set to 0.01). Then, for an
invoice with N line items, the reasonable accumulated error range for its total GST
amount should be:
Expected_Tolerance = N * Max_Line_Error
For example: * 1-line invoice: Expected_Tolerance = 1 * 0.01 = 0.01 * 200-line
invoice: Expected_Tolerance = 200 * 0.01 = 2.00
3.2. Proposed Tolerance Rule
We recommend adopting the following two-tier tolerance validation logic:
Tier 1: Absolute Fixed Tolerance (for all invoices)
Set a very small absolute tolerance, such as 0.05 or 0.10. This tolerance is
used to capture extremely small rounding differences that may exist even in
single-line invoices, or as a minimum tolerance for all invoices.
Logic: If |System Calculated GST - Invoice GST| <= 0.05 , then
allow posting.
Purpose: To handle the most common, extremely small rounding differences
and avoid excessive blocking.
Tier 2: Dynamic Tolerance Based on Number of Line Items (for multi-line
invoices)
For invoices that fail Tier 1 validation, further check their number of line
items.
Logic: If Number of Invoice Line Items > 1 AND |System
Calculated GST - Invoice GST| <= (Number of Invoice Line
Items * Max_Line_Error_Threshold) , then allow posting.
Max_Line_Error_Threshold : This value needs to be calibrated based on
actual conditions. Based on your description, you can try setting it to 0.01.
This means we allow a maximum accumulated error of 0.01 per line.

Purpose: Specifically addresses reasonable larger discrepancies in multi-line
invoices due to accumulated rounding.
3.3. Comprehensive Validation Process
When Concur transmits invoice data to SAP, the system should perform the following
validation logic:
Calculate Expected GST: Based on the invoice's non-taxable amount and the 15%
GST rate, calculate the system's expected total GST amount (to multiple decimal
places).
Get Invoice GST: Obtain the total GST amount provided by the supplier from the
invoice data transmitted by Concur.
Calculate Difference: Difference = |System Expected GST - Supplier
Invoice GST| .
Validate Tier 1 Tolerance:
If Difference <= 0.05 , then Allow Posting.
Validate Tier 2 Tolerance:
If Difference > 0.05 , then further check:
Get the number of line items N on the invoice.
Calculate the dynamic tolerance upper limit:
Dynamic_Tolerance_Limit = N * 0.01 .
If Difference <= Dynamic_Tolerance_Limit , then Allow Posting.
Block Posting: If both of the above validations fail, then Block Posting, mark the
invoice as an exception, and trigger a manual review process.
3.4. Examples
Example 1: 200-line invoice, Difference 2.00
Difference = 2.00
Tier 1 validation: 2.00 > 0.05 , failed.
Tier 2 validation: N = 200 ,
Dynamic_Tolerance_Limit = 200 * 0.01 = 2.00 .
2.00 <= 2.00 , passed. Allow Posting.
Example 2: 1-line invoice, Difference 2.00
Difference = 2.00
Tier 1 validation: 2.00 > 0.05 , failed.
Tier 2 validation: N = 1 , Dynamic_Tolerance_Limit = 1 * 0.01 =
0.01 .
2.00 > 0.01 , failed. Block Posting.
Example 3: 5-line invoice, Difference 0.03
Difference = 0.03
Tier 1 validation: 0.03 <= 0.05 , passed. Allow Posting.
3.5. Key Calibration Parameters
Max_Line_Error_Threshold (0.01): This value is critical. It represents the
maximum acceptable rounding error per line in GST calculation. You need to
analyze the actual situation of supplier invoices and historical data to confirm if
0.01 is a reasonable upper limit. If the supplier's rounding logic is stricter, this value
can be smaller.
Absolute Fixed Tolerance (0.05): This value can be adjusted based on your
tolerance for extremely small discrepancies. It captures cases where there are very
few line items but still minor rounding differences.
4. Implementation Recommendations
Data Analysis and Calibration: Before implementation, conduct a detailed
analysis of historical invoice data, especially invoices with GST discrepancies.
Statistically analyze the distribution of actual GST discrepancies for different
numbers of line items to precisely calibrate Max_Line_Error_Threshold and
the absolute fixed tolerance.
System Implementation: Implement the two-tier validation rules described above
in the Concur to SAP interface or in SAP's custom validation logic. Ensure that the
number of line items on the invoice can be accurately obtained.
Exception Workflow: Establish a clear exception handling workflow. For invoices
blocked from posting, automatically notify relevant personnel for manual review
and processing.
Continuous Monitoring and Optimization: After go-live, continuously monitor
GST discrepancy situations and the number and reasons for blocked invoices.
Regularly review and adjust tolerance parameters based on actual operational
feedback and business needs to achieve the optimal balance.
Supplier Communication: For suppliers who frequently have unreasonable
discrepancies, it may be necessary to communicate with them to understand the
calculation and rounding logic of their invoicing systems, and to guide them
towards adopting calculation methods that are more compliant or transparent.

entry.title: Custom query VIEW_ALL_PLANNING_BOARD_TECHNICIANS by current user?
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

we’re trying to configure User Policy Groups - Read Model Filters in SAP FSM to limit the technicians shown on the Planning Board, based on who is currently logged in. We found a working example for activities, but can't get the same logic to work for technicians.

This query for VIEW_ALL_WFM_ACTIVITIES_LIST_ITEMS works well: { "query": { "bool": { "must": [ { "match": { "activity.responsibleId.raw":"{{currentUser.id}}" } } ] } } }

We tried to adapt the same approach for VIEW_ALL_PLANNING_BOARD_TECHNICIANS with "unifiedPerson.id":"{{currentUser.id}} or  "unifiedPerson.id.raw" … but it doesn’t return any results, even though a technician with matching ID exists.

Business case 1: We want to restrict technicians on the Planning Board so that a user only sees themselves (their own schedule), not the entire team. Ideal for field technicians accessing the Planning Board directly.

Business case 2: We want users to see only technicians that belong to the same business partner (unifiedPerson.businessPartner)

We found a way to use orgLevel but it's not really what we're looking for. 

Any pointers, examples, or working configurations would be greatly appreciated!
Thanks in advance.

Filip

entry.title: Migration to RISE with SAP with Selective Data Transition (SDT)
entry.content:
Why Flexibility is the Key to a Successful Transformation 

In an era where digital transformation is not just a goal but a necessity, many companies face a central question: How can we successfully move to SAP S/4HANA and position the entire organization for the future? For many, the answer lies in RISE with SAP – SAP’s comprehensive cloud offering. It combines software, infrastructure, and services in one integrated package. But the journey is complex: processes, data, and systems must be securely migrated to the new environment without operational risks. 

This article explains how the Selective Data Transition (SDT) approach supports this transition – enabling companies to migrate to the cloud quickly, efficiently, and flexibly. 

RISE with SAP: More Than Just Cloud Infrastructure 

RISE with SAP provides businesses with a clear outlook for the future: a flexible, cloud-based ERP environment with access to cutting-edge technologies like artificial intelligence, automation, real-time analytics, and process intelligence. But it’s about more than technological innovation – RISE represents a holistic approach to business transformation. 

Key benefits for companies include: 

  • Flexible operating models: SAP S/4HANA Cloud – Public Edition, SAP S/4HANA Cloud – Private Edition or SAP S/4HANA On-Premise in a modular concept 
  • Standardization & harmonization: Consolidate and optimize processes and systems 
  • Scalable innovation: Rapid access to new features and innovations via the cloud 

The goal is to pave the way for an agile, scalable, and innovation-ready ERP environment. But technological capabilities alone aren’t enough to overcome the associated challenges. This is where Selective Data Transition (SDT) comes in: a selective migration approach that combines the strengths of the traditional Greenfield and Brownfield methods – and delivers results faster. 

 

The Challenges of Traditional Migration Approaches 

When migrating to SAP S/4HANA, the how is just as important as the what. One of the main challenges is to ensure a seamless transition without disrupting ongoing operations. This forces companies to ask: Should we completely redesign the ERP system from scratch (Greenfield), or will a technical system conversion (Brownfield) suffice? 

A closer look at the traditional approaches can help guide the decision: 

  • Greenfield approach: This method involves a complete redesign of the system landscape. Companies have the opportunity to redefine their processes – especially useful when outdated or inefficient structures exist. However, this approach requires significant investment and involves long implementation times. 
  • Brownfield approach: This involves a technical conversion of the existing system. Most processes and data are retained, allowing for faster implementation. However, it carries the risk of carrying over legacy issues that can hinder innovation. However, it carries several risks: legacy issues from previous system versions may be transferred unchanged, limiting future innovation. Moreover, since there is typically no selective data cleansing or reduction, large systems tend to remain large – leading to increased complexity, higher operational costs, and limited agility in the long run. 

Beyond these two traditional paths, Selective Data Transition (SDT) has emerged as a forward-looking solution – bridging the gap between Greenfield and Brownfield and enabling companies to shape their transition to SAP S/4HANA in an innovative and sustainable way. 

 

Selective Data Transition: A Hybrid Migration Approach 

Selective Data Transition (SDT) combines the best of both worlds. But what exactly makes this approach so unique? 

  • Selective data transfer: Companies can decide exactly which data and processes to carry over to the new system. This allows for outdated or redundant information to be discarded, while retaining business-critical data. At the same time, this approach enables companies to systematically migrate the data that will be relevant for future machine learning and AI applications – a crucial step toward becoming a truly data-driven enterprise. In addition, the data can be freely adopted to enable further optimizations as described below. 
  • Business process optimization: Unlike pure system conversion, SDT allows for a detailed analysis and targeted improvement of existing processes. Companies can eliminate inefficient workflows and implement best practices – actively shaping their digital transformation. 
  • Harmonization and consolidation: Many organizations operate multiple SAP systems in parallel – often due to mergers or international expansion. SDT enables the consolidation of various systems into one unified S/4HANA environment, simplifying the IT landscape and reducing costs. 
  • Tailored to business needs: Every company has specific requirements for its IT environment. SDT enables the creation of customized migration paths that align with individual business models and structures. 

This hybrid approach provides the flexibility companies need to build a sustainable, future-proof SAP landscape. 

 

RISE with SAP and SDT: A Powerful Combination 

Cloud migration is a central component of many companies’ digital strategies. A strategic move via SDT offers several key advantages: 

  • Flexibility and tailored strategy: While a full Greenfield approach often implies a radical change, SDT enables a gradual transition to the cloud. Companies can align their migration roadmap with their business strategy. 
  • Cost efficiency through cloud technologies: Moving to RISE with SAP allows companies to benefit from a subscription-based model instead of large one-time investments. At the same time, selective migration reduces effort and associated costs. 
  • Business continuity: One major challenge of migration projects is minimizing downtime. SDT enables a controlled transfer of data and processes, allowing business operations to continue largely uninterrupted during the transition. 
  • Future-proof through innovation: RISE with SAP provides access to modern cloud technologies such as AI-powered analytics, automation, and IoT integration. With SDT, companies can strategically integrate these innovations into their existing IT landscape – without risking a complete system overhaul. 

 

Ready for the Future 

The question is no longer if you should migrate to SAP S/4HANA, but how. Companies looking to leverage the full potential of the cloud should define their migration strategy now. The SDT approach offers a flexible, future-ready way to modernize IT landscapes and strategically position businesses for what lies ahead. 

Those who act early will be best positioned to fully capitalize on the competitive edge offered by digital transformation. 

Discover more: 

Watch the full webinar recording now and discover how Selective Data Transition can accelerate your move to RISE with SAP – with real-world insights, practical guidance, and strategic takeaways: https://youtu.be/sRRwpi7VtcE?list=PLpQebylHrdh7WMU3RRilp-kdAkqmb8MQx

entry.title: Is Selective Data Transition the Best Fit for Your SAP S/4HANA Migration? Key Aspects to Consider
entry.content:
Choosing the Right SAP S/4HANA migration path: Brownfield, Greenfield, or Selective Data Transition?

As SAP ECC customers prepare for the transition to S/4HANA, they are faced with a crucial decision: which migration approach best suits their business needs? Broadly, there are three main transition paths: Brownfield, Greenfield, and Selective Data Transition. Each approach comes with distinct benefits and trade-offs, making it essential to understand their key differences before choosing the best fit.

On one end of the spectrum is the Brownfield approach, also known as system conversion. This involves taking the existing ECC system and converting it as-is to S/4HANA. On the other extreme is the Greenfield approach, which entails a completely new implementation of S/4HANA. This approach allows organizations to start from scratch, redefining business processes, implementing new configurations, and adopting SAP best practices. What’s left in the middle is the selective data transition approach. It is basically a hybrid of greenfield and brownfield approach.

Selective Data Transition: The hybrid approach with two key variations

Selective Data Transition (SDT) combines elements of both Greenfield and Brownfield approaches. SDT allows businesses to retain critical processes and data while optimizing and modernizing their system.

Within SDT, there are two key variations:

  • Shell Conversion: This approach creates a 'shell' of the existing ECC system, bringing over configurations while allowing for selective process and data migration. It provides an opportunity to eliminate obsolete custom code and streamline operations. Customers choose this option when they want to bring over most of existing processes and configurations from ECC to S/4HANA, but still need to change certain processes and select specific data for migration
  • Mix-and-Match: This scenario starts with a new S/4HANA system (similar to greenfield) but selectively integrates certain ECC processes and configurations. This approach enables businesses to retain essential elements while still leveraging SAP’s latest innovations. Customers choose this option when they prefer a new S/4HANA implementation, but still would like to bring over some of the processes and configurations from ECC.

Greenfield, Brownfield or Selective? Key factors in choosing the right approach

Several factors influence which transition path is most suitable:

Speed of transition
System conversion (brownfield) is typically the fastest approach. Shell conversion also offers a fast transition, although the decision-making process on which configurations to carry over may slow down the overall timeline. In a mix-and-match scenario, the transition begins with a greenfield setup, but critical processes and configurations from ECC can be selectively integrated. Identifying and incorporating the necessary processes into the new system requires additional effort, making the transition speed moderate to low. A completely new implementation generally has the slowest transition speed.

Clean-core adoption
Brownfield conversions tend to carry over legacy modifications, therefore clean core adoption is low. With the shell conversion approach, there is an opportunity to identify and remove obsolete custom code from the system, which makes the potential for clean core adoption relatively higher. Greenfield and mix-and-match approaches offer the best opportunity for a streamlined, clean-core system.

Process Transformation
Brownfield keeps existing processes largely intact, while SDT and Greenfield enable varying degrees of business process redesign.

Data Scope
In a greenfield scenario, only essential data—typically master data and open transactions—is migrated to the new system. Brownfield migrations include the full transfer of historical and transactional data. Shell conversion and mix-and-match approaches support selective data migration, allowing organizations to decide which data is relevant to bring over. The scope of historical data migration can be further drilled down based on modules, objects, purchase orders etc.

Go-Live Strategy
Brownfield requires a big-bang cutover, whereas SDT and greenfield allow for a phased rollout, reducing risk and disruption.

 

Category

Brownfield

SDT: Shell Conversion

SDT: Mix-and-Match

Greenfield

Speed

High

Moderate to High

Moderate to Low

Low

Clean Core Adoption

Low

Moderate

High

Very High

Process Transformation

Low

Moderate

High

Very High

Go Live Strategy

Big Bang

Phased / Big Bang

Phased / Big Bang

Phased / Big Bang

Scope of Data

Full data (historical and transaction)

Configuration, master data, open transactions, selective closed transactions

New system with selectively migrated data from ECC

Master data and open transactions

 

Big Bang vs. Wave-Based (phased) approach

Once the customer decides on the overall approach, it’s equally important to choose the right go-live strategy. In general, the big bang approach is recommended as it reduces the complexity of managing parallel systems and allows for a faster migration. However, in some cases, migrating everything in a single weekend simply isn't feasible—this could be due to the size of the database, the number of users, the complexity of interfaces, or other technical limitations.

In such scenarios, a wave-based approach may be more appropriate. This strategy divides the migration into smaller, manageable phases, reducing the immediate impact on the business. However, it is more complex because it requires careful planning to manage data consistency across systems, coordinate between multiple go-lives, and ensure that both legacy and new systems can operate in parallel without disruptions. While wave-based migrations demand more effort, they offer greater flexibility for organizations with highly integrated or large-scale environments.

Managing historical data during a migration

When considering a move to SAP S/4HANA, the decision to include historical data is a crucial factor in determining whether the Selective Data Transition (SDT) approach is the right fit. While a new implementation may be suitable when only master data and open items are required, many organizations choose the Selective Data Transition approach because they rely on existing customizations and critical business processes that they want to preserve.

SDT makes it possible to selectively migrate historical data—whether by time range (e.g., last one or two years), active company codes, or even down to specific objects—giving customers fine-grained control over what data is transferred. Unlike a system conversion, SDT works at the database level, enabling high-volume data migrations with minimal downtime. This means transaction data, such as purchase orders, can be carried over exactly as they existed in the source system. Importantly, there’s no need to archive all historical data in advance; only the data needed is selected and migrated.

Archiving remains relevant for compliance-heavy industries like aerospace or pharmaceuticals, but SDT provides the flexibility to bring over just the right amount of history without the overhead of full pre-archiving.

Governance, scope, and teamwork: Foundations of a successful SDT project

Strong and consistent project governance is one of the most important success factors in a Selective Data Transition (SDT) project. Unlike system conversions, which are typically led by IT teams, SDT involves a broader range of stakeholders. In addition to IT and Basis teams, business users, functional experts, and shared service centers all play a crucial role. These teams are often distributed across different locations, making clear communication and alignment essential.

A well-defined and stable scope is critical. Changing the scope during testing can lead to confusion, delays, and rework. That’s why it’s important to define the scope early, stick to it, and ensure that all data filtering, transformation, and validation activities are aligned with it.

Equally important is having a dedicated project team on both the customer and service provider sides. This should include a project manager, IT architect, and business owners for each key area. Change management also plays a big role in project success—mid-project upgrades, new rollouts, or added functionalities should be carefully reviewed and ideally avoided.

With clear governance, a committed team, and a stable scope, SDT projects have a much greater chance of success.

Planning for downtime in Selective Data Transition projects

SDT approach typically requires a full shutdown of the source ECC system during cutover. This is because data is migrated at the table level, making it necessary to lock users, stop batch jobs, and disable interfaces to ensure consistent and accurate data extraction.

A typical SDT go-live weekend begins with ramp-down activities on Friday afternoon: locking users, halting interfaces, and preparing systems. Data migration and validation take place over Saturday, followed by ramp-up activities and final testing on Sunday. The goal is always to return the system to the business by Monday morning—minimizing disruption to operations.

Rehearsals are a key success factor here. Mock cutovers allow teams to test and refine every detail of the process, from technical steps to stakeholder coordination. Performance tuning also plays a major role—each phase of the downtime, from data extraction to validation, needs to be optimized for speed without sacrificing data quality.

With the right preparation, a full data migration can be completed over a single weekend—bringing the organization live on S/4HANA without extended interruptions to core business processes.

Finding the right fit for your SAP S/4HANA migration

Choosing the right approach for your SAP S/4HANA migration depends on various factors, including your organization’s goals, timelines, and existing system configurations. While the Greenfield and Brownfield approaches offer different advantages, Selective Data Transition provides a middle ground, allowing businesses to retain key data and processes while modernizing their systems.

By considering key factors such as project scope, data migration needs, and governance, businesses can make a more informed decision about which approach aligns best with their objectives. Whether opting for Selective Data Transition or another path, a thoughtful approach to migration can help ensure a smoother transition to SAP S/4HANA.

Watch our recorded webinar where industry experts discuss how to choose the best migration approach for your business. Gain valuable insights on best practices, key success factors, and practical tips for a smooth transition to SAP S/4HANA.

Watch the full video now: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Jtthwxq35vU&t=2892s

entry.title: Re: edit a job history by import without inserting a new...
entry.content:
Hi @user116 

Recommended practise is download the existing job history date range report with mandated fields (effective start date, event reason, transaction sequence and User ID) and other required fields. 

Prepare job history upload file in csv format, in which do not change "effective start date" and "transaction sequence", so system will identify on which existing user record you are updating the change. Include the header and value for which you are replacing with new value. 

Try to validate the file, make sure maintain the proper effective start date, preceding cells which has "0" has to be in text format. If validation is successful then proceed with the import. 

Try to follow the above transaction in your test environment if the result is as per your expectation then proceed the same in production instance. 

 

Thanks 

Arun

entry.title: Dump CONVT_NO_NUMBER due to 3405744
entry.content:
Note 3405744 is implemented in the system. When an attempt is made to call up a document, the include LEINRF91 runs through.

The system dumps at position 154. " "P" cannot be interpreted as a number ".

The statement in 154 :

LOOP AT bet WHERE ebeln = pot-ebeln AND ebelp = pot-ebelp AND ( vgabe = 2 OR vgabe = 3 ). "^3405744

 

the entry of the parked document is selected with VGABE = P and leads to programme termination (because no number).

 

The following instruction would be better:

LOOP AT bet WHERE ebeln = pot-ebeln AND ebelp = pot-ebelp AND ( vgabe = "2" OR vgabe = "3" ). ---

entry.title: Re: Deploying a Fiori Adaption Project application to the...
entry.content:
Hello @Puffblende 

Thanks for sharing the context and screenshots. You’re on the right path. Deploying a Fiori Adaptation Project in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud can be tricky due to restrictions compared to on-premise.

 Problem

You've deployed an adaptation project via SAP Business Application Studio (BAS), but the app is not selectable in the “Manage Launchpad Space” app in Public Cloud.

 Root Cause

In S/4HANA Public Cloud, once a Fiori Adaptation Project is deployed

  • It must be assigned to a business catalogue and business role.
  • Only then will it appear in the Manage Launchpad Spaces/Pages app.

What To Do Next,

  1. Verify Deployment Was Successful
    • Open the “Maintain App Variants” app in your S/4HANA system.
    • Confirm your adapted app appears in the list with status Deployed.
  2. Assign to Business Catalogue
    • Use the “Maintain Business Catalogues” app (role required).
    • Add your adapted app to a business catalogue (can be custom).
    • Ensure this catalogue is assigned to a business role that’s assigned to your user.
  3. Manage Launchpad App
    • Now go back to “Manage Launchpad Spaces”.
    • You should be able to find and add your adapted app via its App ID or title.

Important Notes

  • You cannot directly select or expose the app in the Launchpad until it’s part of a business role (authorisation model in Public Cloud).
  • If you only deployed it but skipped the catalogue/role assignment, it will not appear in any launchpad config apps.

Helpful SAP Documentation:

  • SAP Help – Adapting SAP Fiori UIs (SAP S/4HANA Cloud)

Thank you and kind regards

Chuma

entry.title: OData-Service API_BUSINESS_PARTNER
entry.content:
Hallo,

für ein aktuelles Nebenprojekt haben wir uns für die Nutzung des SAP Standard OData-Services API_BUSINESS_PARTNER und in Kombination mit der SAP Eventing-Technologie (SAP Event Mesh) entschieden.
In unserem S/4HANA-Entwicklungssystem (Release 2023 / Support-Package Level 1) konnten wir von der Zahl her genau 8 BP-bezogene "Events" bzw. "Topic Identifier" festmachen, wovon nur 5 in die engere Auswahl geschafft haben.

Aber bei einer erweiterten Recherche über das Internet (ChatGPT) wurden uns weitere ca. 6 BP-bezogene "Events" bzw. "Topic Identifier" genannt, die in der Werteliste in unserem Entwicklungssystem nicht angeboten werden.

Beispiele:

- Business Partner Relationship Created 
- Business Partner Relationship Changed
- Business Partner Relationship Deleted
- Business Partner Address Created 
- Business Partner Address Changed
- Business Partner Address Deleted

Was könnte der mögliche Grund (etwas nicht aktiviert / falsches Release bzw. Support-Package Level ?) sein oder war die Antwort von ChatGPT inkorrekt (passiert auch gelegentlich) ?

Ein schnelles Feedback würde uns sehr helfen und würden uns natürlich auch darüber sehr freuen.

Herzlichen Dank im Voraus,

R. Saimun

entry.title: What's new around our SAP Community Groups
entry.content:
It has been a while since our last update on our SAP Community Groups. 

Since our last update we have launched a new Integration group, and the Application Development and SAP Builders group were merged into the new Application Development and Automation group.  We have also started many new location groups, you can check them out to see if there is a group near you. 

We re-launched the Coffee Corner group, as the new Community Corner group. 
The Coffee Corner discussions are of course still alive, and we added a new blog post board to the group. Come join us there, the topics are not always necessarily work or community-related. @Marian_Zeis for instance recently shared how he built a raised bed with free barn wood 😊 

And sometimes, we have groups that for various different reasons need to be sunset. 
The following groups below will all be sunset as of June 30th, 2025: 
Career Corner

Galway
Kiel

SAP for Aerospace and Defense
SAP for Automotive
SAP for Banking
SAP for Chemicals
SAP for Consumer Products
SAP for Defense and Security
SAP for Healthcare
SAP for High Tech
SAP for Higher Education and Research
SAP for Insurance
SAP for Life Sciences
SAP for Media
SAP for Mill Products
SAP for Mining
SAP for Professional Services
SAP for Public Sector
SAP for Retail
SAP for Sports and Entertainment
SAP for Telecommunications
SAP for Travel and Transportation
SAP for Wholesale Distribution

The way the sunset process works, is that we will create a new read-only "Archived Content" area of the Community that we will move the entire group and its content to. This means that all the content of the inactive groups will still remain in the public community, and can be found both by search and by navigation. Sunsetting groups will therefore also not affect any community member's reputation, as all content you have published to any of the sunset groups will remain in your profile. 

The images below are from our test system, to give you an idea of what the Archived Content are of the Community will look like. 
New "Archived Content" area added to the navigation bar: 

Archived Groups will follow the same navigation pattern as the Groups area of the Community:  

Content will be marked as read-only: 

One of the most important aspects of this sunset process, is that it will allow us to be able to play more with the groups concept. We can start new groups, test concepts together with you, and see if they work. If the groups don't get the traction, or engagement, we had hoped for, we simply move them to the Archived Content area and move on to try something else instead. We are open for ideas 🙂

entry.title: #COE#RFL01 VAT refund XML file for Belgium
entry.content:
Hello,

In the EU and in certain conditions, a company can ask for a refund of VAT paid to another member of EU.

The conditions are explained in:

  •  Directive 2008/9/EC
  • Update with :  Implementing Regulation 79/2012

Each country has a portal to declare invoice and VAT to refund.

I actually work for a Belgian company and Belgium use portal “Intervat”.
https://finance.belgium.be/en/taxpayers-established-in-belgium-foreign-vat-refund

In Intervat there are two ways to declare a refund:

  • Populate it manually
  • Upload an xml file

Xml file structured is explained with an XSD file “NewVatRefund-in_v0_9.xsd”
https://finances.belgium.be/fr/E-services/Intervat/documentation-technique

I searched for a way in SAP to generate this XML, but I am not sure that SAP is compliant.

I always look at some customizing points and apps.

SCCUI 103491 is used to declare Tax code to refund.

To add information of “expense” code we can use SCCUI 103490 or 103492.

App “VAT refund (UE)” create an overview of the FI documents that are relevant to VAT refund. Select “Create Reporting Portfolio” allowed to use app “Prepare Electronic Tax return”.

And in app “Prepare Electronic Tax Return” we can select a return type “0003 – VAT refund” and, for me, that should permit to create the XML file that Intervat need.

Do we agree or not ?

If SAP is not compliant, there is another way in SAP Public Cloud (2-landsape) to generate this XML file ?

Best regards,

Teddy.

#COE#RFL01

entry.title: Re: Subcontracting value should be added in material pric...
entry.content:
Hi @kranti_babhulkar,

as @Chuma said, what you are describing should be possible, as long as you have different material numbers for the purchased material and the material which you provide to your subcontractor.

The process would look like this:

Subcontracting info record for the procured material (SG23) with service charges of 1.90 EUR per piece:

Bill of Material for SG23 with materials to be provided to the subcontractor:

Cost estimate of the procured material (SG23) including service charge and material costs:

This results in a standard price of 315 EUR per 100 pieces.

Subcontracting purchase order with service charge of 1.90 EUR per piece according to purchase info record:

Transfer posting to subcontracting stock:

 

Goods receipt of SG23 with consumption of provided materials:

 

Resulting accounting document with the value of 315 EUR for 100 procured pieces:

If you want to use the same material number to provide it to the subcontractor and to procure from the subcontractor just differentiating by valuation type/batch, I'm afraid this is not possible.

If you have any additional questions, feel free to ask.

Best regards
Marvin Fuehrer

entry.title: SAP Certified Associate - Implementation Consultant - SAP SuccessFactors Full Cloud/Core Hybrid
entry.content:
Hello,

Please be informed of the upcoming retirement of our certification C_HRHFC - Implementation Consultant - SAP SuccessFactors Full Cloud/Core Hybrid. SAP Certified Associate: It will be retire it by June 30, 2025.
The learning content materials is still available on help pages on SAP Learning Hub until SAP Learning Hub  shutdown.
All learners impacted by this retirement will receive an additional 12-month validity from the retirement date until their certification loses its validity. That means all certificates valid on June 30, 2025, automatically stay valid until the end of June 2026 without needing a stay certified assessment.
The recommended alternative is HRH98 Integrating SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central with SAP HCM for SAP S/4HANA which leads only to a completion badge.
The expired certificates will remain in the learner's history linked to your SAP Universal ID.

Kind regards
Margit

If you use the application job framework, you yourself cannot write the job log, that is taken care of by the application job framework.

entry.title: Webinar: Enterprise Architecture Education | Modern Approaches with SAP LeanIX
entry.content:
Join us for our upcoming webinar, Enterprise Architecture Education: Modern Approaches with SAP LeanIX, which is part of the SAP University Alliances 2025 Webinar Series

About: Digital transformation is reshaping how companies operate, and Enterprise Architecture is vital for aligning business goals with innovative IT strategies. If you want to discover how a modern approach to EA can help organizations thrive—and see how these principles are being incorporated into academic courses—don’t miss our webinar: Enterprise Architecture Education – Modern Approaches with SAP LeanIX, happening on May 14, 2025. We’ll explore how SAP LeanIX, a leading Enterprise Architecture tool, is used in academic programs to prepare students for real-world IT challenges.

Speakers: Professor João Rebelo Moreira will share his firsthand experience of integrating SAP LeanIX into his Architecture of Information Systems course, providing valuable insights into bridging the gap between theory and practice.

Audience: This webinar is open to all professors and students. Feel free to share the invitation with colleagues whom you believe would enjoy joining!

Save your spot, register now: Webinar Registration - Zoom

Learn more about LeanIX Academic Edition: https://www.leanix.net/en/enterprise-architecture/academic-edition
